claims.flip_evidence_text: Clause (xii) excludes from the definition of Company Material Adverse Effect any Effect resulting from 'the public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger... in each case only to the extent resulting from the execution and delivery of this Agreement and the Merger'; the proviso permitting disproportionate effects to be considered applies only to clauses (ii) through (ix); and under subsection (b), effects on the ability to consummate the Merger are excluded if resulting from clause (xii).
claims.notes: The disproportionate adverse effect carve-back does not apply to clause (xii), which explicitly excludes announcement/pendency effects from both prongs (a) and (b) of the MAE definition.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Company Material Adverse Effect” means any Effect that, individually or in the aggregate, has had, or would be reasonably expected to have, a material adverse effect on: (a) the business, financial conditions, results of operations or assets of the Acquired Companies, taken as a whole, other than any such Effect resulting from (i) any change or prospective change in the market price or trading volume of the Common Stock or the credit ratings of the Company (but not any Effect underlying such change to the extent that such Effect would otherwise constitute a Company Material Adverse Effect), (ii) general economic conditions in the United States or any other country or region in the world (or changes in such conditions) or conditions in the global 4 economy generally (or changes in such conditions), (iii) changes in conditions in the financial markets, credit markets or capital markets in the United States or any other country or region in the world, including (A) changes in interest rates or credit ratings in the United States or any other country; (B) changes in exchange rates for the currencies of any country; or (C) any suspension of trading in securities (whether equity, debt, derivative or hybrid securities) generally on any securities exchange or over-the-counter market operating in the United States or any other country or region in the world, (iv) changes in applicable Laws (or any interpretation or enforcement thereof) that are binding on any of the Acquired Companies, (v) changes in GAAP or regulatory accounting requirements (or any interpretation or enforcement thereof), (vi) geopolitical conditions (or changes in such conditions) or acts of war, outbreak of hostilities, sabotage, military actions, terrorism, civil unrest, protests, or riots (including any escalation or general worsening of any such acts of war, outbreak of hostilities, sabotage, military actions, terrorism, civil unrest, protests, or riots) in the United States or any other country or region in the world, (vii) changes in regulatory, legislative or political conditions in the United States or any other country or region in the world, (viii) the existence, occurrence or continuation of any force majeure event, including any earthquakes, floods, mudslides, hurricanes, tropical storms, nuclear incidents, pandemics, epidemics, or disease outbreaks (including COVID-19), quarantine restrictions, severe weather conditions, tsunamis, tornados, volcanic eruptions, fires or other natural disasters in the United States or any other country or region in the world (including any COVID-19 Measures), (ix) changes in conditions generally affecting the principal industry in which the Acquired Companies operate, (x) any failure by the Company to meet any analysts’ estimates or expectations of the Company’s revenue, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations for any period, or any failure by the Company or any of its Subsidiaries to meet any internal budgets, plans or forecasts of its revenues, earnings or other financial performance or results of operations (it being understood that the Effects giving rise to or contributing to such failures may constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been or will be, a Company Material Adverse Effect), (xi) any matter disclosed on Section 4.9(b) of the Company Disclosure Letter, (xii) the execution and delivery of this Agreement and compliance by the Company with the terms of, or any action taken or not taken by any of the Acquired Companies that is expressly required by, this Agreement, or any action taken or not taken by or at the written request of a Purchaser Party, or the public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger, departures of officers or employees, changes in relationships with suppliers or customers or other business relations, in each case only to the extent resulting from the execution and delivery of this Agreement and the Merger; provided that any Effect resulting from any of the matters described in clauses (ii) through (ix) may be taken into account in determining whether or not there has been, or would reasonably expected to be, a Company Material Adverse Effect if, but only if, such Effect has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Acquired Companies, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies of a similar size in the industry in which the Acquired Companies operate (in which case, only the incremental disproportionate adverse impact may be taken into account in determining whether there has occurred a Company Material Adverse Effect), or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Merger or comply with its obligations under this Agreement, other than any such Effect resulting from any of the matters described in the immediately preceding clause (xii). (Pages 8-9)

Question:
Announcement, pendency or consummation of deal (Y/N)

Llama answer:
Yes, the announcement, pendency, or consummation of the deal is a Company Material Adverse Effect, but only to the extent that it results in a disproportionate adverse effect on the Acquired Companies, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies of a similar size in the industry in which the Acquired Companies operate.

Canonical claims:
- claim-9602738bde51: Yes, the announcement, pendency, or consummation of the deal is a Company Material Adverse Effect, but only to the extent that it results in a disproportionate adverse effect on the Acquired Companies, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies of a similar size in the industry in which the Acquired Companies operate.

whether there has occurred a Company Material Adverse Effect), or (b) the ability of the Company to consummate the Merger or comply with its obligations under this Agreement, other than any such Effect resulting from any of the matters described in the immediately preceding clause (xii). (Pages 8-9)

Question:
Announcement, pendency or consummation of deal (Y/N)

Llama answer:
Yes, the announcement, pendency, or consummation of the deal is a Company Material Adverse Effect, but only to the extent that it results in a disproportionate adverse effect on the Acquired Companies, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies of a similar size in the industry in which the Acquired Companies operate.

Canonical claims:
- claim-9602738bde51: Yes, the announcement, pendency, or consummation of the deal is a Company Material Adverse Effect, but only to the extent that it results in a disproportionate adverse effect on the Acquired Companies, taken as a whole, as compared to other companies of a similar size in the industry in which the Acquired Companies operate.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to evaluate a claim against a contract excerpt, question, and model answer (Llama answer).

 Contract excerpt defines "Company Material Adverse Effect" (CMAE). It has two main parts: (a) effects on business/financial conditions/etc., and (b) ability to consummate the Merger.

 The question is: "Announcement, pendency or consummation of deal (Y/N)"

 The Llama answer says: Yes, it is a CMAE, but only to the extent it results in a disproportionate adverse effect...

 The claim is exactly the same as the Llama answer.

 Let's analyze the contract text carefully.

 Under part (a), there are exclusions (i) through (xii). Clause (xii) excludes:
 "the execution and delivery of this Agreement and compliance by the Company with the terms of, or any action taken or not taken by any of the Acquired Companies that is expressly required by, this Agreement, or any action taken or not taken by or at the written request of a Purchaser Party, or the public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger, departures of officers or employees, changes in relationships with suppliers or customers or other business relations, in each case only to the extent resulting from the execution and delivery of this Agreement and the Merger"

 So the announcement/pendency/consummation of the deal is explicitly excluded from CMAE under part (a), UNLESS... wait, let's look at the proviso.

 The proviso says: "provided that any Effect resulting from any of the matters described in clauses (ii) through (ix) may be taken into account... if... disproportionate adverse effect..."

 Note that the proviso applies to clauses (ii) through (ix). Clause (xii) is NOT included in the proviso. So the disproportionate effect exception does NOT apply to clause (xii).

 Therefore, announcement/pendency/consummation is entirely excluded from CMAE under part (a).

 What about part (b)? Part (b) says: "the ability of the Company to consummate the Merger or comply with its obligations under this Agreement, other than any such Effect resulting from any of the matters described in the immediately preceding clause (xii)."

 So under part (b), effects on ability to consummate are CMAEs, EXCEPT those resulting from clause (xii). Since announcement/pendency/consummation is in clause (xii), it is excluded from part (b) as well.

 Therefore, the announcement, pendency, or consummation of the deal is NOT a CMAE, and the disproportionate effect exception does not apply to it.

 The Llama answer (and claim) says Yes, it is a CMAE, but only to the extent of disproportionate effect. This is incorrect. The correct answer should be No (or Yes with caveats that don't include disproportionate effect? No, it's excluded entirely).

 Wait, let me re-read carefully.

 Clause (xii): "the execution and delivery of this Agreement and compliance by the Company with the terms of, or any action taken or not taken by any of the Acquired Companies that is expressly required by, this Agreement, or any action taken or not taken by or at the written request of a Purchaser Party, or the public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger, departures of officers or employees, changes in relationships with suppliers or customers or other business relations, in each case only to the extent resulting from the execution and delivery of this Agreement and the Merger"

 So "public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger" is excluded from CMAE under (a).

 Proviso: "provided that any Effect resulting from any of the matters described in clauses (ii) through (ix) may be taken into account..."

 Since (xii) is not in (ii)-(ix), the disproportionate effect carve-back does not apply.

 Under (b): "other than any such Effect resulting from any of the matters described in the immediately preceding clause (xii)." So excluded from (b) too.

 Therefore, the claim is false. The announcement/pendency/consummation is NOT a CMAE, and certainly not subject to a disproportionate effect test.

 Is the claim load-bearing? The claim is the entire answer provided by Llama. It asserts that it IS a CMAE subject to disproportionate effect. This is the core of the answer. If we flip it, we get the correct information. So yes, it is load-bearing.

 Flip evidence text should be the text from the contract that shows the exclusion. Specifically, clause (xii) and the proviso limiting the disproportionate carve-back to clauses (ii)-(ix), and part (b) excluding clause (xii).

 Let me construct the flip evidence text. It should be a direct quote or close paraphrase from the contract showing why the claim is wrong.

 Key text:
 - Clause (xii) excludes "the public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger... in each case only to the extent resulting from the execution and delivery of this Agreement and the Merger"
 - Proviso: "provided that any Effect resulting from any of the matters described in clauses (ii) through (ix) may be taken into account..." (implying (xii) is not included)
 - Part (b): "other than any such Effect resulting from any of the matters described in the immediately preceding clause (xii)."

 So the flip evidence text could be something like:
 "Clause (xii) excludes from the definition of Company Material Adverse Effect under both (a) and (b) any Effect resulting from 'the public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger,' and the proviso allowing disproportionate effects to be taken into account applies only to clauses (ii) through (ix), not clause (xii)."

 Or more directly quoting:
 "other than any such Effect resulting from ... the public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger ... in each case only to the extent resulting from the execution and delivery of this Agreement and the Merger"; and "provided that any Effect resulting from any of the matters described in clauses (ii) through (ix) may be taken into account"; and under (b) "other than any such Effect resulting from any of the matters described in the immediately preceding clause (xii)."

 The user asks for correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true.
 This is false. The claim says it IS a CMAE (with disproportionate effect). It is not.

 Wait, could it be partially true? Is there any sense in which announcement/pendency/consummation is a CMAE? No, it's explicitly excluded from both (a) and (b). So false.

 load_bearing_label: yes. Because it's the main assertion of the answer.

 flip_evidence_text: required since load_bearing=yes.

 notes: optional short note. I should note that the disproportionate effect carve-out applies only to clauses (ii)-(ix), excluding (xii).

 Let me double-check if "pendency or consummation" is covered by clause (xii). Clause (xii) mentions "execution and delivery of this Agreement", "compliance... with the terms of... this Agreement", "public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger". It doesn't explicitly say "pendency or consummation". However, the question asks about "Announcement, pendency or consummation of deal". The Llama answer groups them together. 

 Does the contract cover pendency/consummation? "execution and delivery of this Agreement" and "compliance by the Company with the terms of... this Agreement" and "public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger". Pendency might be covered by "compliance with the terms of this Agreement" or "execution and delivery". Consummation might be covered by "the Merger". But regardless, the Llama answer treats them as covered by clause (xii) and applies the disproportionate test. The claim is about the Llama answer's content.

 Actually, looking at the claim text: "Yes, the announcement, pendency, or consummation of the deal is a Company Material Adverse Effect, but only to the extent that it results in a disproportionate adverse effect..."

 This is false because:
 1. The announcement is explicitly excluded under (xii).
 2. The disproportionate effect exception does not apply to (xii).
 3. Under (b), effects on ability to consummate are excluded if from (xii).

 So the claim is false.

 Is there any nuance? Could "consummation" be separate? Under (b), "the ability of the Company to consummate the Merger" is part of the definition, but then excluded if resulting from (xii). If the consummation itself is hindered by something else, maybe. But the claim specifically links announcement/pendency/consummation to the disproportionate effect test, which is wrong.

 Therefore:
 correctness_label: false
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: [quote showing exclusion under (xii) and that disproportionate carve-back only applies to (ii)-(ix) and exclusion under (b)]
 notes: The disproportionate adverse effect exception applies only to clauses (ii) through (ix), not clause (xii) which covers announcement of the Agreement or Merger; clause (xii) exclusions also apply to part (b).

 Let me format the flip evidence text carefully. It should be concise but accurate.

 "Clause (xii) excludes from the definition of Company Material Adverse Effect any Effect resulting from 'the public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger... in each case only to the extent resulting from the execution and delivery of this Agreement and the Merger'; the proviso permitting disproportionate effects to be considered applies only to clauses (ii) through (ix); and under subsection (b), effects on the ability to consummate the Merger are excluded if resulting from clause (xii)."

 This looks good.

 Wait, I need to make sure I'm returning exactly the required fields for each claim. The prompt says "For each claim return: - correctness_label... - load_bearing_label... - flip_evidence_text... - notes..."

 Since there's only one claim (claim-9602738bde51), I should return the evaluation for it.

 Let me check if the claim could be partially true. The Llama answer says "Yes... but only to the extent...". If the announcement/pendency/consummation were covered by (ii)-(ix), then it would be partially true or true depending on interpretation. But since it's in (xii), it's completely false. There is no circumstance under the contract where announcement/pendency/consummation is a CMAE due to disproportionate effect. So false is correct.

 One final check: Does the proviso "clauses (ii) through (ix)" include (xii)? No, clearly not. (xii) is after (xi). The proviso is before (xii) in the text? Let me check the text structure.

 Text: "(xi) any matter disclosed... (xii) the execution and delivery...; provided that any Effect resulting from any of the matters described in clauses (ii) through (ix) may be taken into account..."

 Wait, the proviso comes AFTER clause (xii) in the text? Let me look.

 The excerpt says:
 "(xi) any matter disclosed on Section 4.9(b) of the Company Disclosure Letter, (xii) the execution and delivery of this Agreement and compliance by the Company with the terms of, or any action taken or not taken by any of the Acquired Companies that is expressly required by, this Agreement, or any action taken or not taken by or at the written request of a Purchaser Party, or the public announcement of this Agreement or the Merger, departures of officers or employees, changes in relationships with suppliers or customers or other business relations, in each case only to the extent resulting from the execution and delivery of this Agreement and the Merger; provided that any Effect resulting from any of the matters described in clauses (ii) through (ix) may be taken into account in determining whether or not there has been, or would reasonably expected to be, a Company Material Adverse Effect if, but only if, such Effect has a disproportionate adverse effect on the Acquired Companies..."

 So yes, the proviso comes after clause (xii) but explicitly says "clauses (ii) through (ix)". It does not include (xii). This is a common structure in MAE definitions: list specific carve-outs, then have a general carve-back for some but not all of them. Here, the carve-back is explicitly limited to (ii)-(ix).

 Therefore, the claim is definitively false.

 Final output format. I'll use a structured format, likely JSON or markdown list. The prompt doesn't specify exact format, but since it says "For each claim return:", I'll provide a clear structure.

 Actually, looking at similar tasks, usually they want something like:

 claim-9602738bde51:
   correctness_label: false
   load_bearing_label: yes
   flip_evidence_text: "..."
   notes: "..."

 Or perhaps a JSON object. I'll provide a clean markdown or JSON-like structure. Since the user listed the fields with dashes, I'll return them in a clear block.

 Let me write it out.
